news_summary: Today's positive human rights news highlights progress in addressing the global housing crisis, advances in understanding human behavior, and insights into the social factors influencing criminality.
news_summary_fr: Les bonnes nouvelles du jour en matière de droits de l'homme mettent en lumière les progrès réalisés dans la lutte contre la crise mondiale du logement, les avancées dans la compréhension du comportement humain et les éclairages apportés sur les facteurs sociaux qui influencent la criminalité.
news_summary_es: Las noticias positivas de hoy en materia de derechos humanos ponen de relieve los avances en la lucha contra la crisis mundial de la vivienda, los progresos en la comprensión del comportamiento humano y las conclusiones sobre los factores sociales que influyen en la delincuencia.

articles.title: Housing crisis takes centre stage at World Urban Forum in Baku
articles.summary: Now is the time to place housing at the heart of sustainable development. That was the message delivered on Monday by UN Secretary General António Guterres to participants at a global forum on urban sustainability under way this week in Baku.
articles.content:
His video address set the tone for the 13th session of the World Urban Forum (WUF13), which comes at a critical moment as cities grapple with deepening inequalities, rapid urbanisation and a growing climate crisis.

Organised by UN‑Habitat in partnership with the Government of Azerbaijan, the forum, which has drawin a record number of participants, officially opened today following Sunday’s ceremonial events, which included the first-ever World Leaders Summit in the history of the gathering.

Housing rises up the global agenda

Tweet URL

UN‑Habitat officials describe the summit as a major step in elevating housing and urbanisation to the highest political level.

Mr. Guterres stressed that housing directly affects people’s health, education and future opportunities.

“As Secretary-General, I am proud that the Pact for the Future and the Doha Political Declaration recognize housing as a priority,” he added, referring respectively to a landmark 2024 UN agreement aimed at strengthening global cooperation and safeguarding the needs of future generations, and the declaration adopted at last year’s World Social Summit, focused on advancing inclusive development.

“Housing is a human right, and fundamental for human dignity,” Mr. Guterres emphasized, calling on participants to make affordable housing a “shared reality.”

The Secretary‑General also warned that the crisis is no longer confined to low‑income countries. “No society is immune, from fast‑growing cities in the developing world, to advanced economies with high rents and rising homelessness,” he said.

No simple fixes for a global crisis

President of the UN General Assembly Annalena Baerbock said in a separate video message that there are no straightforward solutions.

“Building more houses is necessary, but it will not be enough on its own,” she stressed.

She called for a more comprehensive approach – one that links housing with infrastructure, climate resilience, financing and urban planning – and underlined that sustainable urban development is impossible without the participation of local communities, civil society and residents themselves.

Cities under pressure

According to UN estimates, around 1.5 million people move into cities every week. By 2050, more than two‑thirds of the world’s population will be living in urban areas, with nearly 90 per cent of that growth concentrated in Africa and Asia.

At the same time, informal settlements are continuing to expand: in developing countries, more than 30 per cent of urban residents live in slums and neighbourhoods lacking adequate infrastructure.

Tweet URL

Participants have gathered at the forum to explore solutions to these and related challenges, including access to basic services, climate resilience and more effective urban governance.

Azerbaijan and Somalia currently serve as co‑chairs of the UN‑Habitat Intergovernmental Working Group on the resolution ‘Adequate Housing for All’ for the 2025–2026 term, coordinating UN Member States’ efforts and developing global recommendations on safe, sustainable and affordable housing.

Welcoming participants, President Ilham Aliyev of Azerbaijan described his country as “the place where East meets West”, noting that this interplay of “old and new” is reflected in Azerbaijani architecture. “Today, organizing this global gathering, we [have again] demonstrated our strong commitment to urban planning and our agenda to make life of the people better”, he added.

Record turnout amid extreme weather

Executive Director of UN‑Habitat Anacláudia Rossbach described the level of interest in the forum as unprecedented.

“Never before has a forum of this nature brought together such a vast and diverse global audience,” she said. “Despite the intense rainfall and thunderstorms that have affected parts of Azerbaijan in recent days, we have come together here in Baku with resilience, determination and a shared sense of purpose,” Rossbach added.

On 17 May, Baku and the Absheron Peninsula recorded a historic 103 millimetres of rainfall – equivalent to 523 per cent of the average monthly total for May – with heavy rain continuing uninterrupted for nine hours.

As a result of the severe weather, the World Leaders Summit and several other events had to be moved from the Olympic Stadium to the Baku Congress Center. The opening of the largest Urban Expo in WUF history was also delayed.

Spanning 3.5 hectares, the exhibition brings together more than 217 organisations from 66 countries. Governments, universities, international organisations, private companies and startups are presenting projects focused on affordable housing, digital technologies, climate resilience and inclusive urban development.

UN News Photo

Why so many spirals?

At the centre of the Expo stands the UN‑Habitat pavilion, dedicated to the WUF13 theme: Housing the World. Its concept is built around the image of a spiral – a symbol of the interconnectedness of people, nature and cities.

Speaking to UN News, UN‑Habitat Chief of Communications Katerina Bezgachina explained that the idea emerged from reflections on what “home” means to people.

“We started thinking about what home means for a human being. And we realized that home is also our planet and our universe,” she said.

The spiral appears throughout nature – from galaxies to the shell of a snail, which “always carries its home with it”.

Visitors move through a series of interconnected “spirals”, each devoted to a different theme. One explores the global housing crisis, while showcasing solutions from around the world.

“We did not want to show only problems. We wanted to show that there are many solutions – people just do not always know about them,” the UN‑Habitat communications chief explained.

Another section traces the path from the 1948 Universal Declaration of Human Rights to the first Habitat conference, held in Vancouver in 1976, after which UN‑Habitat was established.

The pavilion’s design also echoes the modern skyline of Baku and its iconic landmarks – the Heydar Aliyev Center and the Flame Towers, the latter featured on the official WUF13 logo. Inside, a dedicated UN‑Habitat Arena will host 17 thematic sessions from 19 to 22 May.

UN News is in Baku covering the Summit throughout the week. Follow along here.
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article reports on the World Urban Forum's significant global focus on housing as a fundamental human right and a key element of sustainable development. It highlights high-level political commitment, broad international participation, and comprehensive approaches linking housing with infrastructure, climate resilience, and urban planning. The forum's scale, record attendance, and emphasis on practical solutions to a global housing crisis affecting billions underscore its broad positive impact and substance.
articles.personality_title: Global leaders unite in Baku to tackle housing crisis at World Urban Forum
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Cities around the world are growing quickly, with millions moving to urban areas every week. This growth brings challenges like housing shortages, rising rents, and expanding slums. The World Urban Forum (WUF13) in Baku gathers experts and leaders to find ways to make cities safer, fairer, and more sustainable.

**What happened** – The 13th World Urban Forum opened in Baku, Azerbaijan, with a record number of participants. UN Secretary-General António Guterres delivered a video message emphasizing that housing is a human right and essential for health and opportunity. The forum included the first-ever World Leaders Summit, showing strong political commitment to affordable and sustainable housing. Despite heavy rain and storms in Baku, the event continued, featuring a large Urban Expo with projects from 217 organizations across 66 countries.

**Impact** – This forum is unique because it puts housing at the center of global discussions on sustainable development. Leaders stressed that solving the housing crisis requires more than building houses; it needs planning that connects housing with infrastructure, climate protection, and community involvement. The large turnout and high-level meetings mark a new step in treating housing as a priority worldwide, not just in poor countries but also in rich ones facing homelessness and high rents.

**What's next step** – The forum will continue through the week with sessions on housing solutions, urban planning, and climate resilience. Azerbaijan and Somalia, as co-chairs of a UN working group, will help guide global efforts to create safe, affordable housing. The ideas and agreements made here aim to influence policies and projects worldwide in the coming years.

**One-sentence takeaway** – At the World Urban Forum in Baku, global leaders and experts came together to raise housing as a top priority, aiming to create better, fairer cities for everyone.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les dirigeants mondiaux unis à Bakou pour relever le défi du logement au Forum urbain mondial
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les villes se développent rapidement, avec des millions de personnes qui s’y installent chaque semaine. Cette croissance provoque des problèmes comme le manque de logements, la hausse des loyers et l’expansion des bidonvilles. Le 13e Forum urbain mondial (WUF13) à Bakou réunit des experts et des dirigeants pour trouver des solutions pour rendre les villes plus sûres, justes et durables.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le 13e Forum urbain mondial a débuté à Bakou, en Azerbaïdjan, avec un nombre record de participants. Le secrétaire général de l’ONU, António Guterres, a souligné dans un message vidéo que le logement est un droit humain essentiel à la santé et aux opportunités. Le forum a accueilli le tout premier Sommet des dirigeants mondiaux, montrant un fort engagement politique pour un logement abordable et durable. Malgré de fortes pluies et orages, l’événement a continué avec une grande exposition urbaine rassemblant 217 organisations de 66 pays.

**Impact** – Ce forum est unique car il place le logement au cœur des discussions mondiales sur le développement durable. Les dirigeants ont souligné que résoudre la crise du logement demande plus que construire des maisons : il faut aussi relier le logement aux infrastructures, à la protection du climat et à la participation des communautés. La forte participation et les rencontres de haut niveau marquent une nouvelle étape pour considérer le logement comme une priorité mondiale, pas seulement dans les pays pauvres, mais aussi dans les pays riches confrontés à l’itinérance et aux loyers élevés.

**Prochaines étapes** – Le forum se poursuit toute la semaine avec des sessions sur les solutions au logement, l’urbanisme et la résilience climatique. L’Azerbaïdjan et la Somalie, co-présidents d’un groupe de travail de l’ONU, guideront les efforts mondiaux pour créer des logements sûrs et abordables. Les idées et accords du forum devraient influencer les politiques et projets dans le monde entier dans les années à venir.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Au Forum urbain mondial de Bakou, dirigeants et experts du monde entier ont placé le logement en priorité pour bâtir des villes meilleures et plus justes pour tous.
articles.personality_title_es: Líderes mundiales se unen en Bakú para enfrentar la crisis de vivienda en el Foro Urbano Mundial
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las ciudades están creciendo rápidamente, con millones de personas mudándose cada semana. Este crecimiento trae problemas como la falta de viviendas, el aumento de los alquileres y la expansión de barrios informales. El 13º Foro Urbano Mundial (WUF13) en Bakú reúne a expertos y líderes para buscar soluciones que hagan las ciudades más seguras, justas y sostenibles.

**Qué pasó** – El 13º Foro Urbano Mundial se inauguró en Bakú, Azerbaiyán, con una asistencia récord. El Secretario General de la ONU, António Guterres, envió un mensaje en video destacando que la vivienda es un derecho humano fundamental para la salud y las oportunidades. El foro incluyó la primera Cumbre de Líderes Mundiales, mostrando un fuerte compromiso político con la vivienda asequible y sostenible. A pesar de fuertes lluvias y tormentas, el evento continuó con una gran Expo Urbana que reunió a 217 organizaciones de 66 países.

**Impacto** – Este foro es único porque coloca la vivienda en el centro de las discusiones globales sobre desarrollo sostenible. Los líderes destacaron que resolver la crisis de la vivienda requiere más que construir casas; necesita planificación que conecte viviendas con infraestructura, protección climática y participación comunitaria. La gran asistencia y las reuniones de alto nivel marcan un nuevo paso en el reconocimiento de la vivienda como prioridad mundial, no solo en países pobres sino también en ricos que enfrentan falta de hogar y altos alquileres.

**Próximo paso** – El foro continuará durante la semana con sesiones sobre soluciones de vivienda, planificación urbana y resiliencia climática. Azerbaiyán y Somalia, como copresidentes de un grupo de trabajo de la ONU, ayudarán a guiar los esfuerzos globales para crear viviendas seguras y asequibles. Las ideas y acuerdos logrados aquí buscan influir en políticas y proyectos en todo el mundo en los próximos años.

**Frase clave** – En el Foro Urbano Mundial en Bakú, líderes y expertos globales unieron fuerzas para poner la vivienda como prioridad y construir ciudades mejores y más justas para todos.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a large, interconnected spiral structure symbolizing a home, blending architectural elements inspired by Baku’s Flame Towers and Heydar Aliyev Center with abstract representations of diverse urban housing forms, surrounded by gentle silhouettes of people and nature harmoniously intertwined, all rendered in soft, natural earth tones and muted blues.

articles.title: Scientists think they’ve cracked the mystery of human right-handedness
articles.summary: A new study suggests humans became overwhelmingly right-handed because of two major evolutionary shifts: walking on two legs and developing much larger brains. Researchers found that as human ancestors evolved, their right-hand preference steadily intensified — transforming a mild tendency into one of humanity’s most distinctive traits.
articles.content:
One of the biggest mysteries in human evolution has long puzzled scientists: Why are humans so overwhelmingly right-handed? Around 90% of people across cultures prefer using their right hand, a level of dominance not seen in any other primate species. Researchers have spent decades studying the brain, genetics, and development behind handedness, but the reason humans became so strongly right-handed has remained unclear.

Now, a new study led by researchers at the University of Oxford points to two major evolutionary milestones: walking upright and the dramatic growth of the human brain.

The research, published in PLOS Biology, was conducted by Dr. Thomas A. Püschel and Rachel M. Hurwitz from Oxford's School of Anthropology and Museum Ethnography, together with Professor Chris Venditti from the University of Reading. The team analyzed data from 2,025 monkeys and apes representing 41 different primate species.

Using Bayesian modeling that considered how species are evolutionarily related, the researchers tested several major theories about the origins of handedness. They examined factors including tool use, diet, habitat, body size, social structure, brain size, and movement patterns.

Upright Walking and Brain Expansion

Humans initially stood apart from every other primate in the analysis. However, that changed once researchers added two key traits into their models: brain size and the ratio between arm length and leg length, which is commonly used as a marker of bipedal movement.

After accounting for those features, humans no longer appeared to be such an evolutionary exception. The findings suggest that the combination of upright walking and larger brains may explain why humans developed such an unusually strong preference for the right hand.

The study also allowed researchers to estimate likely handedness in extinct human ancestors. Their results suggest that early hominins such as Ardipithecus and Australopithecus probably showed only mild right-hand preferences, similar to what is seen in modern great apes today.

That pattern appears to strengthen significantly with the emergence of the genus Homo. Species including Homo ergaster, Homo erectus, and Neanderthals likely had increasingly strong right-hand preferences, eventually leading to the extreme dominance seen in modern humans.

The Curious Case of the "Hobbit" Species

One species stood out from this trend: Homo floresiensis, the small-bodied species often nicknamed the "hobbit" because of its tiny size. Researchers predicted that this species had a much weaker right-hand bias.

According to the team, that finding fits the broader evolutionary pattern. Homo floresiensis had a relatively small brain and retained physical adaptations for both climbing and upright walking, rather than being fully specialized for bipedal movement.

The researchers believe the evidence points to a two-stage evolutionary process. First, walking upright freed the hands from locomotion, creating new pressures that favored more specialized and asymmetric hand use. Later, as human brains became larger and more complex, the preference for the right hand grew much stronger and more widespread.

Dr. Thomas A. Püschel, Wendy James Associate Professor in Evolutionary Anthropology at the University of Oxford, said: "This is the first study to test several of the major hypotheses for human handedness in a single framework. Our results suggest it is probably tied to some of the key features that make us human, especially walking upright and the evolution of larger brains. By looking across many primate species, we can begin to understand which aspects of handedness are ancient and shared, and which are uniquely human."

Why Left-Handedness Still Exists

The study also raises new questions for future research. Scientists still do not fully understand why left-handedness has persisted throughout human evolution or how human culture may have helped reinforce right-handedness over time.

Researchers are also interested in whether similar limb preferences found in animals such as parrots and kangaroos could point to deeper evolutionary patterns shared across very different species.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in understanding a fundamental aspect of human biology and evolution—right-handedness—linking it to key evolutionary milestones. This discovery has broad significance for anthropology, neuroscience, and human identity, providing meaningful insight into what makes humans unique. The study is detailed, based on extensive data, and advances knowledge with potential implications for future research on human evolution and behavior.
articles.personality_title: Scientists uncover why most people are right-handed
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long wondered why about 90% of humans use their right hand more than their left, a pattern not seen in other primates. Understanding this could reveal important clues about human evolution.

**What happened** – A new study by researchers at the University of Oxford and the University of Reading analyzed data from over 2,000 monkeys and apes across 41 species. They used advanced models to explore how different traits, like brain size and walking on two legs, relate to hand preference. Their research showed that two key changes—walking upright and having larger brains—helped humans develop a strong right-hand preference.

**Impact** – This study is the first to test many ideas about handedness together and explains why humans are different from other primates. It suggests that early human ancestors had only a slight right-hand preference, which grew stronger as brains got bigger and walking on two legs became common. This helps explain a unique part of being human: our strong right-handedness.

**What's next step** – Scientists want to learn why left-handedness still exists and how culture might influence hand use. They are also curious if similar patterns in animals like parrots and kangaroos connect to evolution in other species.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Human right-handedness likely developed through two big evolutionary changes: standing up to walk and growing larger brains, shaping a key human trait.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques expliquent pourquoi la plupart des humains sont droitiers
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques se sont longtemps demandé pourquoi environ 90 % des humains utilisent davantage leur main droite, un phénomène absent chez les autres primates. Comprendre cela pourrait révéler des indices importants sur l'évolution humaine.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Une nouvelle étude menée par des chercheurs de l'Université d'Oxford et de l'Université de Reading a analysé les données de plus de 2 000 singes et grands singes de 41 espèces. Ils ont utilisé des modèles avancés pour étudier comment des traits comme la taille du cerveau et la bipédie sont liés à la préférence manuelle. Leur recherche montre que deux changements majeurs – la marche debout et l'augmentation de la taille du cerveau – ont aidé les humains à développer une forte préférence pour la main droite.

**Impact** – Cette étude est la première à tester plusieurs hypothèses sur la latéralité manuelle en même temps et explique pourquoi les humains diffèrent des autres primates. Elle suggère que les premiers ancêtres humains avaient une légère préférence pour la main droite, qui s'est renforcée avec la croissance du cerveau et la marche bipède. Cela aide à comprendre une caractéristique unique de l'humain : sa forte droiterie.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques veulent comprendre pourquoi la gaucherie existe toujours et comment la culture influence l'usage des mains. Ils s'intéressent aussi à voir si des animaux comme les perroquets et les kangourous montrent des tendances similaires.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La droiterie humaine s'est probablement développée grâce à deux grands changements évolutifs : la bipédie et l'agrandissement du cerveau, façonnant un trait clé de l'humain.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren por qué la mayoría de las personas son diestras
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos han preguntado durante mucho tiempo por qué alrededor del 90 % de las personas prefieren usar la mano derecha, algo que no ocurre en otros primates. Entender esto puede dar pistas importantes sobre la evolución humana.

**Qué pasó** – Un nuevo estudio realizado por investigadores de la Universidad de Oxford y la Universidad de Reading analizó datos de más de 2,000 monos y simios de 41 especies diferentes. Usaron modelos avanzados para ver cómo características como el tamaño del cerebro y caminar en dos patas están relacionadas con la preferencia de mano. Descubrieron que dos cambios importantes—caminar erguidos y tener cerebros más grandes—ayudaron a que los humanos desarrollaran una fuerte preferencia por la mano derecha.

**Impacto** – Este estudio es el primero que prueba varias ideas sobre la lateralidad manual juntas y explica por qué los humanos son diferentes a otros primates. Sugiere que los primeros humanos tenían una preferencia derecha leve que se fortaleció con cerebros más grandes y la marcha bípeda. Esto explica un rasgo único de los humanos: usar mucho la mano derecha.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos quieren saber por qué la zurdera sigue existiendo y cómo la cultura influye en el uso de las manos. También estudian si animales como loros y canguros muestran patrones similares que puedan relacionarse con la evolución.

**Resumen en una frase** – La preferencia por la mano derecha en humanos probablemente surgió por dos grandes cambios evolutivos: caminar erguidos y el aumento del tamaño del cerebro, formando un rasgo humano clave.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a stylized upright primate silhouette with one hand prominently extended and glowing softly to symbolize right-handedness, set against a backdrop of a gently growing brain shape blending into the horizon, using natural earth tones and soft light to evoke evolution and human uniqueness.

articles.title: Criminals are made, not born: how when you live shapes whether you will break the law
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 18 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01556-yAn analysis of what makes young people more likely to commit crimes tears down the influential assumption that character is the main factor.
articles.content:
Marked by Time: How Social Change Has Transformed Crime and the Life Trajectories of Young Americans Robert J. Sampson Harvard Univ. Press (2026)

‘Greed is the iron cage of our times’ — why nationalism is here to stay

In Stanley Kubrick’s film adaptation of the 1962 novel A Clockwork Orange, gang leader Alex DeLarge is portrayed as an ultraviolent miscreant. Once imprisoned, he is subjected to aversion therapy, which serves only to reinforce the deeply rooted nature of his criminal disposition.

DeLarge is one of cinema’s most memorable villains, and his behaviour aligns with a common explanation for why some people become chronic criminal offenders: ‘bad character’.

In Marked by Time, criminologist Robert Sampson sets out the degree to which academic theory, policing and court practice depend on this idea. He also lays out evidence that it is a fundamentally wrong-headed assumption: it neglects the role of changing historical circumstances in influencing a person’s chances of first being arrested, which subsequently affects whether they become a repeat offender.

Disquieting but effective, Sampson’s book makes a compelling case for rooting out character-based assumptions — and for factoring in historical context — at all stages of the criminal-justice system.

A matter of time

The idea that a person’s character is central to their risk of committing crime is prominent in the field of crime theory. As Sampson concedes, some evidence does indicate that children who are impulsive or lack self-control are more likely to engage in criminal activity as teenagers and adults. A classic study, for example, showed that antisocial behaviour in childhood was a strong predictor of antisocial behaviour in adulthood (L. N. Robins Psychol. Med. 8, 611–622; 1978).

How wealthy tech entrepreneurs seek to shape politics, culture and the future — and why we must resist

Yet Sampson notes that many, if not all, of these long-term studies are based on samples from single historical periods. If character were the key factor in determining who becomes a criminal, its relevance should remain stable across history, even during periods of rapid social change.

To analyse this idea, Sampson taps into a rich trove of data from a longitudinal study called the Project on Human Development in Chicago Neighborhoods. It ran from 1995 to 2024 and was designed to help researchers to understand the historically high crime rates in many US cities in the early 1990s. Researchers collected survey data on the individual, family and neighbourhood circumstances of more than 1,000 young people who were born in Chicago, Illinois, between 1980 and 1995. Sampson was one of its first scientific directors.

What no one knew when the project began was that the mid-1990s marked the beginning of a high decline in crime rates that continued into the mid-2010s — a phenomenon observed in many countries and for which there is no universally accepted explanation. Because the cohort lived through eras with drastically different crime levels, Sampson could explore how the time in which a person lives affects their risk of committing crime and of arrest.

Young people growing up in Chicago took part in a 29-year study of human development.Credit: Scott Olson/Getty

He cites two cases for comparison. Andre and Darnell were both born in the same disadvantaged Chicago neighbourhood, which was racially segregated and had high levels of poverty. But whereas Andre was born in 1980 — and grew up exposed to historically high levels of violence and policing enforcement — Darnell was born in 1995, and his adolescence occurred during a more tranquil period. Andre was arrested by age 19, but Darnell avoided entanglement with the criminal-justice system.

Arresting trends

Sampson argues that differences in the likelihood of arrest across time are a major factor in the boys’ differing life trajectories. The experience of arrest can itself alter a person’s life prospects by disrupting their education, reducing employment opportunities and leading to stigmatization in their community. And the difference in arrest likelihood over time, he shows, cannot be fully explained by demographic, family or neighbourhood conditions, or by self-control: children with high self-control born in the 1980s were just as likely to be arrested as were those with low self-control born in the next decade.
articles.reasoning: The article presents a significant, research-based challenge to the entrenched assumption that criminal behavior is primarily due to inherent character flaws. By highlighting the influence of historical and social context on crime rates and individual life trajectories, it supports more humane and effective criminal justice policies. This has broad societal implications for reducing stigma, improving rehabilitation, and promoting justice reform.
articles.personality_title: New study shows environment shapes crime more than character
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Many people believe that criminals are born with bad character, which makes them more likely to commit crimes. This idea has influenced how police and courts treat offenders for a long time.

**What happened** – A new book by criminologist Robert Sampson challenges this belief. Using data from a 29-year study of young people in Chicago, Sampson found that when and where a person grows up affects their chances of being arrested more than their character traits. For example, two boys from the same poor neighborhood had very different experiences because one grew up during a time of high crime and the other during a safer period.

**Impact** – This research shows that crime is not just about a person’s character but also about the social and historical environment. Arrests can change lives by making it harder to get education and jobs. Understanding this can help create fairer justice systems that focus on changing conditions, not blaming individuals.

**What’s next step** – The findings suggest that police and courts should consider the social context when dealing with crime. Future policies might focus more on improving neighborhoods and reducing arrests for minor offenses to help prevent repeated crime.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A person’s chance of committing crime depends more on when and where they live than on their character alone.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle étude montre que l’environnement influence plus la criminalité que le caractère
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Beaucoup pensent que les criminels naissent avec un mauvais caractère, ce qui les pousse à commettre des crimes. Cette idée a longtemps influencé la façon dont la police et les tribunaux traitent les délinquants.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Un nouveau livre du criminologue Robert Sampson remet en question cette croyance. En utilisant les données d’une étude de 29 ans sur des jeunes de Chicago, Sampson a découvert que le moment et l’endroit où une personne grandit influencent plus ses chances d’être arrêtée que ses traits de caractère. Par exemple, deux garçons du même quartier pauvre ont eu des expériences très différentes parce que l’un a grandi à une époque de forte criminalité et l’autre à une période plus calme.

**Impact** – Cette recherche montre que la criminalité ne dépend pas seulement du caractère d’une personne, mais aussi de son environnement social et historique. Les arrestations peuvent changer une vie en rendant plus difficile l’accès à l’éducation et à l’emploi. Comprendre cela peut aider à créer des systèmes de justice plus justes qui se concentrent sur la transformation des conditions, pas sur la culpabilisation des individus.

**Prochaine étape** – Ces résultats suggèrent que la police et les tribunaux devraient prendre en compte le contexte social dans la gestion de la criminalité. Les futures politiques pourraient mettre davantage l’accent sur l’amélioration des quartiers et la réduction des arrestations pour délits mineurs afin de prévenir la récidive.

**Phrase clé** – La probabilité qu’une personne commette un crime dépend davantage du lieu et du moment où elle vit que de son seul caractère.
articles.personality_title_es: Un nuevo estudio muestra que el entorno influye más en el crimen que el carácter
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Muchas personas creen que los criminales nacen con mal carácter, lo que los lleva a cometer delitos. Esta idea ha influido durante mucho tiempo en cómo la policía y los tribunales tratan a los delincuentes.

**Qué pasó** – Un nuevo libro del criminólogo Robert Sampson cuestiona esta creencia. Usando datos de un estudio de 29 años sobre jóvenes en Chicago, Sampson encontró que el momento y el lugar donde una persona crece influyen más en sus probabilidades de ser arrestada que sus rasgos de carácter. Por ejemplo, dos chicos del mismo barrio pobre tuvieron experiencias muy diferentes porque uno creció en una época de alta criminalidad y el otro durante un periodo más tranquilo.

**Impacto** – Esta investigación muestra que el crimen no depende solo del carácter de una persona, sino también del entorno social e histórico. Los arrestos pueden cambiar vidas al dificultar el acceso a la educación y el empleo. Entender esto puede ayudar a crear sistemas de justicia más justos que se enfoquen en cambiar las condiciones, no en culpar a los individuos.

**Próximos pasos** – Los resultados sugieren que la policía y los tribunales deberían considerar el contexto social al tratar el crimen. Las futuras políticas podrían centrarse más en mejorar los barrios y reducir los arrestos por delitos menores para prevenir la reincidencia.

**Frase clave** – La probabilidad de que una persona cometa un delito depende más del lugar y momento en que vive que de su carácter solamente.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of two intertwined timelines represented by two winding city streets lined with symbolic houses and community landmarks, one street shadowed and fragmented with subtle dark tones symbolizing high crime and arrest in the 1980s, the other bright and peaceful with warm natural colors reflecting social change and reduced crime in the 1990s and beyond, connected by a glowing thread of time that weaves through both, emphasizing how historical context shapes life paths without showing any people.
